We identified the rotator cable in 74.3% of the clinical MRI studies. The cable was most easily and consistently visualized in the coronal and sagittal oblique planes. This increased frequency compare

The realization that the rotator cable can be frequently seen on conventional MRI represents an important first step in understanding its possible biomechanical role in the intact and torn rotator cuf

with rotator cuff abnormality such as fatty
atrophy, tear size, and tear retraction.

The mean cable thickness in our study differed from that in the original papers by Burkhardt et al. [2–4], where the average cable thickness was reported as 0.47 cm. This was most likely because the o

Our study had several limitations. The study was limited by the small number of cadaveric specimens used for imaging, dissection, and histologic evaluation. Artifacts related to the freezing and secti
Conclusion
Our study has shown that the rotator cable is a structure that can be consistently seen on gross anatomic and histologic analysis, arthroscopy, and MRI in the intact rotator cuff.
